Headlines

Sudden Change in the Battle for Fed Chair: Hassett Hints at Exit, Warsh's Winning Probability Soars

White House Economic Advisor Kevin Hassett (Kevin... Hassett indicated that Trump is likely to keep him in his current position, which would disqualify him from the race to become the next Federal Reserve chairman. “From the beginning, he and I have been discussing whether it’s better for me to be in the West Wing or at the Fed,” Hassett said on Fox News’ Sunday Briefing. “I don’t think he’s made a final decision on that.” Trump expressed reservations last week about nominating Hassett to succeed current Fed Chairman Jerome Powell. “To be honest, I actually hope you stay where you are,” he told the National Economic Council director at a White House event. Hassett was once considered a frontrunner alongside Fed Governor Christopher Waller, former Governor Kevin Warsh, and BlackRock executive Rick Rieder. According to sources, it is now seen as a four-way race, with Rieder’s candidacy gaining momentum recently as some believe he may have a better chance of Senate confirmation. Following Trump's speech, traders on the prediction market website Kalshi raised the probability of Warsh getting the job to 60%, while Hassett and Waller's probabilities were only 16% and 14%, respectively. Traders on Polymarket expressed similar sentiments, with Warsh at 60%, Hassett at 15%, and Waller at 13%. Previously, Warsh and Hassett's odds were evenly matched.

MEME Hot Topics

1. A whale was completely liquidated on its DOGE long position, losing $2.2 million

According to Jinse Finance, based on Onchain Lens monitoring, a whale was completely liquidated on its DOGE long position, losing $2.2 million. The whale currently still holds a long position in ETH (15x leverage), with a paper loss of approximately $475,000.

        DeFi Hot Topics

        1. DeFi researcher Ignas: DAO governance structure still has hidden dangers, most project teams do not care about DAO

          2. Vitalik: Now More Optimistic About Native Rollups

          According to Jinse Finance, Vitalik Buterin stated in an article on the X platform, "I am now more inclined towards native rollups than before. One important reason I opposed them before was that the precompile of native rollups had to choose between 'ZK mode' or 'optimistic mode.' ZK-EVM is not mature enough in ZK mode, so if we give L2 two options: 'using proofs with full trust in Ethereum, withdrawals will take 2-7 days' or 'instant withdrawals, but you have to handle the proofs yourself,' L2 will usually choose the former. This is very detrimental to Ethereum's composability and will lead to the adoption of more multisig bridges, etc. But now it seems that Ethereum's timeline for fully accepting ZK on L1 actually matches the real-world timeline for adding native rollup precompiles, so this problem is solved.

data.title: Why I'm not optimistic about Ethereum right now.
data.description:
I'm not optimistic about Ethereum's business development (referring to the long-term growth in user base and number of settlement transactions, which I believe will continue), but rather I don't want to buy it at the current price because its price is too expensive relative to its fundamentals. From these charts, we can create a portrait of Ethereum currently: 1. Active user numbers are rising in waves to new highs (44% higher than the previous cycle peak), and the number of transactions is also at a new high (13% higher than the previous cycle peak). However, the growth rates of these two metrics are not as high as the GMV growth rates of some leading e-commerce platforms.

2. Currently, the monthly transaction fee is only 0.6% of the peak of the previous cycle, and the average transaction fee is only 0.5% of the peak of the previous cycle. This means that its slow growth in users and transaction volume is achieved at the cost of a sharp drop in service prices. When growth comes at the cost of drastic price reductions for products and services, this is not a good sign for any company in any industry.

...

3. If we consider Ethereum as a company that provides block space services, based on December data, its PF (market fee rate) exceeds 2000 times, its PS (price-to-sales ratio) exceeds 10000 times, and its net profit is negative, so there is no price-to-earnings ratio indicator.

3. If we consider Ethereum as a company that provides block space services, based on December data, its PF (market fee rate) exceeds 2000 times, its PS (price-to-sales ratio) exceeds 10000 times, and its net profit is negative, so there is no price-to-earnings ratio indicator.

... The price-to-earnings ratio (P/E ratio) of conventional cloud service companies is around 20-30, while their price-to-sales ratio (P/S ratio) is in the single digits. 4. If Ethereum is considered not a company but a commodity (similar to digital crude oil), its challenge lies in the fact that other public chains and rollups can also provide similar block services (similar to substitutable crude oil). Some might argue that Ethereum's strong decentralization and censorship resistance justify its higher price as a commodity resource, but is it truly worth that much? The previous calls for ETH to replace BTC as a store of value have almost disappeared, as a consensus has largely been reached. Compared to the consensus that BTC equals digital gold, ETH is more like a technology company + specialized cloud service provider, and its commodity positioning makes it more easily substitutable. 5. Crypto-native applications with PMF are almost extinct; this round has seen almost no high-value applications emerge. Insufficient demand and increased supply (the continued growth in the number of rollups and public chains) have led to a severe oversupply of blockchain space, resulting in sluggish growth and even contraction in the public chain sector itself. 6. The grand vision painted by Tom Lee and some domestic VCs—that "Ethereum is the Wall Street of the blockchain, and everything will eventually be on Ethereum"—I believe currently lacks sufficient data and factual evidence to support this story. It lacks concrete logical reasoning and seems more like a sales pitch. Our investment behavior should be based on rationality, not faith. I don't want to take the bait they're offering now. If data and facts gradually corroborate this story, then it's not too late to take it.

data.title: Why do I feel less valuable the more I use AI?
data.description:
A few days ago, I came across a short video of Zhang Wenhong, director of the National Center for Infectious Diseases, speaking at the Hong Kong High-Level Forum on January 10th. He clearly stated, "I refuse to introduce AI into hospital medical record systems." This is because AI without systematic training would fundamentally change the training path for doctors, undermining or damaging the independent diagnostic abilities that young doctors need through traditional training. Zhang Wenhong explained that he himself uses AI, letting it review the cases first. But the key is that, with his more than 30 years of clinical experience, he can immediately spot where the AI ​​is wrong. The problem lies with young doctors. If a doctor starts relying on AI for diagnostic conclusions from their internship, skipping complete clinical reasoning training, they will forever lose a crucial ability: the ability to discern the correctness of AI's diagnostic capabilities. Zhang Wenhong's words, from the perspective of an ordinary AI user, reveal a widely misunderstood reality regarding skills and leverage in the AI ​​era. Over the past year or two, I've witnessed a peculiar "collective anxiety." Interestingly, this anxiety doesn't stem from those unfamiliar with technology; on the contrary, it's more prevalent among the elite groups already proficient in AI: programmers, lawyers, analysts, and self-media personalities. Initially, everyone excitedly believed AI would make them superhuman. But after the brief efficiency frenzy, many fell into a deeper sense of powerlessness: When AI can complete 80% of the work at zero cost, can my remaining 20% ​​of value truly uphold my professional dignity? If an AI can finish two weeks' worth of my code in minutes; if a large model can generate a perfect due diligence report in seconds; if Gemini or Doubao can enable people with no drawing background to produce masterpieces; if GPT can "accurately" read medical examination reports or test reports, then where exactly is the moat protecting human skills? Previously, The Atlantic published an article saying we are entering an era of deskilling; but the other side of the coin is precisely this: AI has not rendered skills useless; it has triggered a dramatic "skill inflation." Skills simply need to be redefined. In an era where execution costs approach zero, AI is a mirror that reveals all. It amplifies not only your efficiency but also the granularity or precision of your cognition. You might feel "useless" because AI has ruthlessly exposed a fact: most of the work you were once proud of was just "bricklaying," execution, and "following orders," rather than "thinking," much less raising and solving problems. The truth about skills in the 21st century is no longer about how many tools you have, but about how much genuine leverage you possess. The comprehensive ability of "macro-level control + micro-level verification" is the true guarantee of a secure future in the AI ​​era. I. Zhang Wenhong's Paradox: 0 times 10 is still 0. There's a widely circulated view in Silicon Valley, but it's often misinterpreted. People say, "AI is a 10x amplifier of productivity." The mathematical implications of this statement are more chilling than the literal meaning. If your current ability is 1, AI will make you 10; if you are 10, AI will make you 100. But if your fundamental understanding of a certain field is 0, then 0 multiplied by 10 is still 0. This is precisely the core of Zhang Wenhong's concern: a young doctor who relies on AI from his internship might have clinical judgment of 0. No matter how powerful AI is, 0 multiplied by any number still results in 0. Even more frightening is that this "0" may not even know it is 0. Zhang Wenhong put it bluntly: "New doctors cannot rely solely on AI for diagnosis." Why? Because even if AI achieves a 95% accuracy rate, the 5% error still needs to be identified and corrected by professional doctors. If doctors lack independent diagnostic skills, how can they detect AI errors? How can they handle complex cases that AI cannot solve? This is what I call the "Zhang Wenhong Paradox," which, on one level, is a chicken-and-egg problem. But on another level, it emphasizes whether humans are using tools or tools are using humans. It reveals the first layer of truth about skills in the AI ​​era: The essence of AI is "probability fitting," while the value of humans lies in "bearing the consequences." In the past, skills often referred to proficient execution, memorizing syntax, reciting legal provisions, and mastering various keyboard shortcuts. However, in the AI ​​era, these hard skills have rapidly depreciated, becoming mere infrastructure. Instead, a more subtle and scarcer ability has emerged: judgment. Judgment, in this context, means understanding the long-term consequences of one's actions. Imagine a scenario: a senior engineer and a novice are simultaneously writing code using AI. The novice only receives code blocks. They cannot judge whether the code has architectural flaws, predict its performance under extreme concurrency, or even whether it's a dead-end solution. The senior engineer, on the other hand, sees not just code, but a path. He knows what tasks to assign to AI, how to verify the results, and, more importantly, where to correct AI errors—and AI is bound to make mistakes. For novices, AI is a black box; they can only hope it outputs the correct answer. For experts, AI is a team of interns with unlimited energy, able to execute tasks precisely. Therefore, the future distinction between experts and ordinary people lies in whether you possess the ability to "verify AI output." Zhang Wenhong can instantly spot where an AI's diagnosis is wrong, not through some mysterious intuition, but through the "meta-ability" accumulated from over thirty years of clinical experience. This ability is precisely what young doctors, whose training has been skipped by AI, lack most. Therefore, without profound professional knowledge as a ballast, AI brings not efficiency, but costly chaos. II. Why are your prompts always "almost there"? Why can some people use AI to solve complex problems, while others can only use it as a chatbot? The problem isn't that you can't write "incantations," but that your mental entropy is too high. Recently, a worrying phenomenon has emerged: people are starting to outsource their thinking to AI. When faced with a problem, instead of breaking it down, they throw a jumbled mess of requirements at the model, then get angry at the mediocre output: "This AI is completely useless." Actually, it's not that AI is stupid, it's that you haven't thought it through. No matter how advanced an AI model is, it's essentially a prediction machine based on "context." Its output quality is strictly limited by the quality of the context you input. This is the modern version of "Garbage In, Garbage Out." The top skills of the 21st century have become "clear expression" and "structured thinking." True masters have already completed a rigorous mental deduction before even opening a dialog box: 1. Define the problem: What core contradiction do I need to solve? 2. Decompose the logic: What are the sub-tasks that make up this big problem? What are the dependencies? 3. Set standards: What kind of result is considered acceptable? For example, before letting AI assist in developing a feature, have you clarified the data flow? Before letting AI write an article, have you built a unique framework of viewpoints? Don't expect AI to do the "from 0 to 1" thinking for you. AI excels at filling in the details (from 1 to 100), but that "1," that core insight, that logical framework, must be provided by you. If you cannot clearly articulate your ideas to human colleagues, you will never get satisfactory results from AI. Clear writing is clear thinking. In the future, programming in natural language will be a universal skill. But this doesn't mean programming will become simpler; rather, it means that the precision of language and logic will become the new code. If your thinking is chaotic, AI will only efficiently amplify that chaos.

III. Breaking Out of the Information Cocoon: Getting Closer to the Essence Than 99% of People

Since AI is trained on massive amounts of existing human data, it inherently carries a huge flaw: mediocre consensus, i.e., regressing to the mean.

If you ask AI for its opinion on health, finance, or history, it will most likely give you a "textbook" answer. These answers are safe and correct, but often extremely mediocre because they simply repeat the most frequently appearing information on the internet.

This leads to the third dimension: the insight to distinguish truth from falsehood.

Knowledge and understanding are two different things.

Knowledge is knowing "you should do it this way"; understanding is understanding "why you should do it this way, and when you shouldn't do it this way." This is precisely the fundamental difference between Zhang Wenhong and young doctors. Young doctors can instantly acquire "knowledge" through AI, such as diagnostic results, medication recommendations, and treatment plans. But Zhang Wenhong possesses "understanding": he knows where the boundaries of this knowledge lie, when to break with convention, and when the "standard answer" given by AI is wrong. In this era of information overload, if you only acquire information through rote learning and algorithmic recommendations, you are essentially just mechanically repeating information in a giant "echo chamber." You don't truly understand how things work. To be smarter than AI, we need to be closer to the essence of things (first principles) than 99% of people. Want to understand business? Don't just read bestsellers and read public accounts; study cash flow, leverage, supply and demand, and human greed. Want to understand health? Don't just believe so-called authoritative guidelines; study the biological mechanisms of metabolism, hormones, and inflammatory responses. When AI gives you a "standardized suggestion," only those who truly understand how the underlying system works can keenly spot its flaws or decisively overturn the AI's suggestion in specific situations. As Zhang Wenhong said: Whether you will be misled by AI depends on whether your own abilities are stronger than AI's. And you can't compete with AI in knowledge; you can only compete in understanding. The competitive advantage of the future belongs to those who dare to question "training data." You need to build your own cognitive system, one that isn't copied, but personally verified through practice, painful feedback loops, and independent thinking. AI represents the average of all human knowledge. If you want to surpass the average, you can't rely solely on AI; you must possess unique insights that AI cannot derive through statistical probability. IV. After the Value of Execution Reaches Zero: From Doer to Acceptance Looking at the bigger picture, history may not repeat itself, but it often rhymes. In the 1980s, the widespread adoption of computers caused panic among accountants and lawyers. Before that, lawyers had to spend days sifting through mountains of files to find a precedent. The advent of electronic retrieval technology simplifies this task to mere seconds. Are lawyers losing their jobs? No. On the contrary, the legal industry has become larger and more complex. Because search has become easier, clients' expectations of lawyers have increased. People no longer pay for "finding precedents," but for "building unique defense strategies based on complex precedents." Similarly, as AI takes over coding, document generation, and basic diagnostics, the role of humans is undergoing a fundamental leap: We are evolving from "craftsmen" to "commanders"; from "doers" to "accepters." In the past, a good engineer might have spent 50% of their time writing code and 50% thinking about architecture. Now, they can spend 90% of their time thinking about architecture, understanding the business, and optimizing the user experience, while delegating the coding work to AI (and having it review it). This means that the upper limit of work complexity has been opened up. An independent developer can now run a company that previously required a ten-person team; a knowledgeable self-media creator can produce a week's worth of content in a single day; a senior doctor (like Zhang Wenhong) can handle a previously impossible number of cases with the assistance of AI. This is the new definition of "skills" in the AI ​​era: It is no longer a single-dimensional "specialization," but a cross-dimensional integration capability. You don't need to lay every brick yourself, but you must know the building's structural mechanics, have aesthetic sense to determine its appearance, and have business acumen to determine where it's most valuable to build. This comprehensive ability of "macro-level control + micro-level verification" is the true guarantee of a secure future in the AI ​​era. The two key capabilities emphasized by Zhang Wenhong essentially mean this: 1. Judging the accuracy of AI diagnoses (microscopic verification) 2. Diagnosing and treating complex and difficult cases that AI cannot handle (macroscopic control) Doctors without these two capabilities can only be considered "AI operators". Conclusion: Only by elevating one's perspective can one enjoy the thrill of defeating a weaker opponent. Returning to the phenomenon mentioned at the beginning: Why do we feel more useless the more we use AI? Because AI deprives us of the right to gain a sense of accomplishment through "labor". Previously, spending three days crafting a beautiful report would make you feel valuable; now, AI can do the same in three seconds, instantly shattering that illusory sense of value. This is indeed painful, but it's also an awakening. AI forces us to confront the most difficult question: Beyond mechanical execution, where does my true intellectual value lie? For those unwilling to think, this is the worst of times. They will become utterly subservient to algorithms, unaware that they are being swallowed by a mediocre information cocoon. But for those full of curiosity, possessing independent thinking abilities, and yearning to explore the essence of things, this is the best of times in human history: all barriers have been lowered. All the ceilings have disappeared. You now possess the most powerful think tank and execution team in human history, on call 24/7. Zhang Wenhong isn't against AI; he opposes skipping the development of fundamental capabilities and directly using AI, outsourcing thinking and metacognition to AI. He himself uses AI with great success because he has thirty years of experience as a foundation. AI is a powerful tool for him; but for young doctors without such experience, AI might be counterproductive, a case of pulling up seedlings to help them grow, or drinking poison to quench their thirst. In the 21st century, skills won't disappear, but they will undergo a brutal refinement. Don't try to compete with AI in "solving problems," compete with AI in "creating problems." When you stop using AI as a tool to help you be lazy, and instead treat it as a super lever that requires your extremely high intelligence to control, guide, and correct mistakes, what you see through AI will no longer be your mediocre self, but an infinitely magnified and powerful super individual.

data.title: A detailed explanation of my country's new anti-money laundering regulations and their impact on the crypto industry.
data.description:
In January 2026, the People's Bank of China, together with eight other departments including the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and the Ministry of Public Security, officially released the "Administrative Measures for Special Preventive Measures Against Money Laundering" (hereinafter referred to as the "Measures"). These Measures, approved by the PBOC's executive meeting on November 17, 2025, will officially come into effect on February 16, 2026. As an important supporting system after the implementation of the new Anti-Money Laundering Law, the Measures, with list management as its core and full-process control as its approach, clarify the scope of application, implementation standards, and liability boundaries of special preventive measures against money laundering. This further improves my country's anti-money laundering, terrorist financing, and weapons of mass destruction proliferation financing prevention and control system, and sets higher requirements for the compliant operation of financial institutions and certain non-financial institutions.

I. Clarification and Establishment of the Anti-Money Laundering "Blacklist System"

The most eye-catching aspect of this new anti-money laundering regulation is the "special preventive measures," which clearly define the anti-money laundering "blacklist system."

The most noteworthy aspect of this new anti-money laundering regulation is the "special preventive measures," namely the clarification of the anti-money laundering "blacklist system."

This blacklist was jointly established by 3+1 entities in my country, namely: (1) the National Counter-Terrorism Leading Group; (2) the Ministry of Foreign Affairs; (3) the People's Bank of China; and (4) the People's Bank of China in conjunction with other relevant national agencies. The following is a detailed analysis by the Sa Jie team. First, the National Counter-Terrorism Leading Group, whose office is the main channel for releasing the list to the public. So far, the lists of terrorist organizations and personnel announced mainly include two categories: (1) lists of East Turkistan terrorist organizations and personnel; and (2) lists of personnel from other terrorist organizations. Currently, the most clearly defined sanctions lists are those issued by the Ministry of Public Security in March 2003, April 2008, and May 2012, targeting the East Turkestan Islamic Movement separatist forces. These lists involve four organizations and 25 individuals. As of now, the publicly available lists are limited, primarily because lists of terrorist organizations and individuals are considered highly sensitive information, and therefore, there may not be publicly available channels for access. You can refer to announcements released by the National Counter-Terrorism Leading Group Office for analysis and information. Secondly, there's the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, which is somewhat unique, primarily involved in fulfilling international legal obligations in accordance with UN Security Council resolutions. For example, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs issued a "Notification on Revising the List of Individuals on the UN Security Council Sanctions Committee on ISIS and Al-Qaeda" on October 7, 2025, regarding cooperation with the UN Security Council's "1267 Committee" on sanctions against ISIS and Al-Qaeda. The specific list can be confirmed by checking the UN website. Other examples, such as the notices issued by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs regarding the implementation of UN Security Council resolutions 1695, 1718, 2397 concerning North Korea and resolution 2402 concerning Iran, all fall within the scope of the new anti-money laundering regulations. Finally, there is the list of organizations and individuals identified by the People's Bank of China alone or jointly with relevant national authorities as having significant money laundering risks and whose failure to take measures may result in serious consequences. This publicly available list is mainly divided into two parts: (1) The international blacklist mainly comes from the Financial Action Task Force (FATF). The latest list can be obtained by logging into the FATF website and finding "High-risk and other monitored jurisdictions" under "publications"; (2) The domestic blacklist comes from the official website of the People's Bank of China. The latest list can be obtained by logging into the official website of the People's Bank of China and finding "Risk Warnings and Financial Sanctions" under "Anti-Money Laundering". In general, banks, payment companies, and other financial institutions, as the main bearers of obligations in counter-terrorism and anti-money laundering, are already quite familiar with the three types of lists mentioned above. This new regulation further clarifies the content of counter-terrorism and anti-money laundering work. II. Is there a way out if you are blacklisted for anti-money laundering? In handling numerous cases of unfreezing large amounts of overseas encrypted assets, our team has found that some overseas institutions may be blacklisted by judicial and law enforcement agencies of various countries and jurisdictions for various reasons (large-scale risky transactions, secondary sanctions imposed on trading partners, questionable fund flows, etc.). So, if you are on my country's anti-money laundering "blacklist," are there any avenues for redress? Article 9 of the new regulations stipulates that, depending on the administrative authority that identifies and publishes the relevant lists, entities on the Chinese version of the "Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Blacklist" can apply to the National Counter-Terrorism Leading Group, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, or the People's Bank of China, respectively. However, the specific implementation will depend on the circumstances. According to Article 9 of the "Administrative Measures for Special Preventive Measures Against Money Laundering," if there is any objection to the list stipulated in Article 2, Paragraph 1 of these Measures, the party concerned may apply for review through the Office of the National Counter-Terrorism Leading Group in accordance with the relevant provisions of the "Anti-Terrorism Law of the People's Republic of China." If there is any objection to the list stipulated in Article 2, Paragraph 2 of these Measures, the party concerned may apply for removal from the list in accordance with the relevant procedures published by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. If there is any objection to the list stipulated in Article 2, Paragraph 3 of these Measures, the party concerned may apply for administrative reconsideration to the department that made the identification; if dissatisfied with the administrative reconsideration decision, they may file an administrative lawsuit in accordance with the law. First, let's clarify one point: if the entity is on either the first category's list announced by the National Counter-Terrorism Leading Group Office or the second category's list of sanctions announced by a UN Security Council resolution, and the new regulations specify remedies such as applying for review and filing for removal, then there's basically no hope of a solution; it's virtually impossible to resolve, and there are virtually no lawyers or professionals who can provide "removal" services. It's worth noting that if the entity falls under the second category of being listed on a UN Security Council sanctions list, whether or not removal can be achieved depends on a UN Security Council resolution, not solely on the decision of my country's regulatory authorities. Second, if the entity is on a list determined solely by the People's Bank of China or jointly with other departments, there is a certain probability of being able to "prove their innocence." It's important to note that entities on such lists have the right to file for administrative review or administrative litigation. Therefore, with the assistance of a lawyer, if the relevant entity insists on collecting objective evidence, they can attempt to seek redress through judicial channels, with a certain probability of success.

III. Who will be affected after the new regulations are officially implemented?

This new regulation is actually a refinement of the "special anti-money laundering preventive measures" introduced in Article 40 of the Anti-Money Laundering Law (2024 Revision), which states that "any unit or individual shall, in accordance with the requirements of the relevant national authorities, take special anti-money laundering preventive measures against the following entities." Clearly, "any unit or individual" includes financial institutions, certain non-financial institutions, as well as enterprises and natural persons.

The Sa Jie team reminds our partners:

First, do not lend your ID card or bank card. If someone uses your ID card to open an account or directly uses your bank card for money laundering activities, once you are included in the aforementioned "blacklist," all your legal personal assets will be restricted from use and transfer.

Even though Article 4 of the new regulations emphasizes the protection of the legitimate rights and interests of bona fide third parties, it still takes a significant amount of time and effort to prove that the "bona fide" standard is met. Secondly, companies must strengthen their due diligence obligations regarding partners. If you are a company involved in cross-border trade or large-scale transactions, you need to pay more attention to vetting your partners than before. Establish a list review system for publicly available channels. Once a partner is found to be on the "blacklist," stop all services and transfers and report to the relevant departments immediately; otherwise, you may be considered an affiliated company and subject to restrictive measures. Thirdly, if you find yourself on the "blacklist" and subject to restrictive measures, consult a lawyer immediately to determine the specific category of the "blacklist," cooperate with the investigation by the relevant departments, do not use or transfer personal assets, and compile records of recent legitimate transactions. Try to lift the "special measures" through the specific category of the blacklist. Fourthly, do not harbor any illusions about virtual currencies. Article 29 of the new regulations explicitly states that funds include proof of asset ownership in electronic or digital form. The use of virtual currencies for money laundering is strictly prohibited. Virtual currencies held by individuals are also within the scope of special anti-money laundering measures. Virtual currencies are never a "firewall" against any illegal or criminal activity. As the new regulations refine the special preventative measures under the Anti-Money Laundering Law, the specific scope of the "blacklist" is becoming increasingly clear. The future trend will undoubtedly be penetrating supervision and transparent transactions. For financial institutions, it is crucial to establish anti-money laundering review mechanisms as soon as possible and cooperate with relevant law enforcement agencies to take special measures. For businesses and individuals, it is essential to operate legally, carefully verify transaction counterparties, and manage their accounts properly to prevent others from using virtual currencies for money laundering activities. In the future, the goal is to achieve an organic unity between compliant business development and risk control, building a solid security defense for the stable and healthy development of the market.

data.title: How will the "spear and shield" arms race in Web3 escalate?
data.description:
Author: imToken

Looking back at 2025, if you feel that on-chain scams are becoming increasingly "in tune with you," this is not an illusion.

With the widespread adoption of LLM, social engineering attacks launched by hackers have evolved from bloated mass emails to "precision targeting": AI can analyze your on-chain/off-chain preferences to automatically generate highly enticing customized phishing content, and even perfectly simulate the tone and logic of your friends on social channels such as Telegram.

It can be said that on-chain attacks are entering a truly industrialized stage. In this context, if the shields we hold are still in the "manual era," security itself will undoubtedly become the biggest bottleneck for the large-scale adoption of Web3.

I. Web3 Security Stuttering: When AI Intervenes in On-Chain Attacks

If the past decade saw Web3 security issues primarily stemming from code vulnerabilities, a significant shift since 2025 is the "industrialization" of attacks, while security measures have not kept pace.

After all, phishing websites can be mass-produced using scripts, and fake airdrops can be automatically and precisely delivered, making social engineering attacks no longer reliant on hackers' deceptive skills but rather on model algorithms and data scale.

To understand the severity of this threat, we can break down a simple on-chain swap transaction. You'll then discover that risks are virtually ubiquitous throughout the entire lifecycle of the transaction, from creation to final confirmation: Before interaction: You might have entered a phishing page disguised as an official website, or used a DApp frontend with a malicious backdoor; During interaction: You might be interacting with a token contract containing "backdoor logic," or the counterparty itself might be a tagged phishing address; During authorization: Hackers often trick users into signing seemingly harmless signatures that actually grant them "unlimited deduction permissions"; After submission: Even if all operations are correct, in the final step of submitting the transaction, MEV scientists may still be lying in wait in the mempool, ready to steal your potential gains through a sandwich attack; This extends beyond Swap to all interaction types, including transfers, Stakes, Mint, etc. Risks are omnipresent in this chain process of transaction creation, verification, broadcasting, on-chain verification, and final confirmation. A problem at any point can render a secure on-chain interaction futile. It can be said that, based on the current account system, even the most secure private key protection cannot withstand a single accidental click by a user; even the most rigorous protocol design can be bypassed by an authorized signature; and even the most decentralized system is most easily breached by "human vulnerabilities." This means a fundamental problem has surfaced—if attacks have entered the stage of automation and intelligence, while defense remains at the level of "human judgment," security itself will become a bottleneck (further reading: "The $3.35 Billion 'Account Tax': When EOA Becomes a Systemic Cost, What Can AA Bring to Web3?"). Ultimately, ordinary users still lack a one-stop solution that can provide security protection for the entire transaction process, while AI has the potential to help us build a security solution for end-users that covers the entire transaction lifecycle, providing a 24/7 defense to protect user assets. II. What can AI × Web3 do? Let's look at the theory and see how the combination of AI and Web3 can reconstruct a new paradigm for on-chain security in the face of this technologically asymmetrical game. First, for ordinary users, the most direct threat is often not protocol vulnerabilities, but social engineering attacks and malicious authorization. At this level, AI plays the role of a 24/7 security assistant. For example, AI can use Natural Language Processing (NLP) technology to identify communication scripts on social media or private chat channels that are highly suspected of being fraudulent: When you receive a "free airdrop" link, for example, the AI ​​security assistant will not only check the website's blacklist, but also analyze the project's social media popularity, domain registration duration, and smart contract fund flow. If the link is backed by a newly created fake contract without any funds injected, the AI ​​will mark a large red cross on your screen. "Malicious authorization" is currently the leading cause of asset theft. Hackers often induce users to sign seemingly harmless signatures that actually grant them "unlimited deduction permissions": When you click to sign, AI will first perform a transaction simulation in the background. It will tell you bluntly: "If you perform this operation, all ETH in your account will be transferred to address A." This ability to translate obscure code into intuitive consequences is the strongest barrier against malicious authorization. Secondly, the protocol and product side can achieve everything from static auditing to real-time defense. In the past, Web3 security mainly relied on periodic manual audits, which were often static and lagging. AI is now being embedded in real-time security processes, much like the well-known automated auditing. Compared to traditional auditing, which requires human experts to spend weeks reviewing code, AI-driven automated auditing tools (such as smart contract scanners incorporating deep learning) can complete logical modeling of tens of thousands of lines of code in seconds. Based on this logic, current AI can simulate thousands of extreme transaction scenarios, identifying subtle "logic traps" or "reentrancy vulnerabilities" before code deployment. This means that even if developers inadvertently leave backdoors, AI auditors can issue warnings before assets are attacked. In addition, security tools like GoPlus can intercept transactions before hackers can act. GoPlus SecNet, for example, allows users to configure on-chain firewalls to check transaction security in real time via an RPC network service. It can proactively intercept risky transactions to prevent asset losses, including transfer protection, authorization protection, protection against fraudulent token purchases, and MEV protection. It can check for risks in transaction addresses and assets before transactions and proactively intercept them if risks are found. Furthermore, I strongly agree with GPT-style AI services, such as providing a 24/7 on-chain security assistant for most novice users to guide them in resolving various Web3 security issues and quickly providing solutions for sudden security incidents. The core value of such systems is not "100% correctness," but rather shifting risk detection from "after the fact" to "during the incident" or even "before the incident." III. Where are the Boundaries of AI × Web3? Of course, it's still a matter of cautious optimism, as always. When discussing the new potential that AI × Web3 can bring to fields such as security, we need to remain restrained. Ultimately, AI is just a tool. It shouldn't replace user sovereignty, nor can it safeguard user assets, much less automatically "intercept all attacks." Its reasonable positioning leans more towards minimizing the cost of human judgment errors without changing the premise of decentralization. This means that while AI is powerful, it is not omnipotent. A truly effective security system must be the result of the combined effects of AI's technological advantages, users' clear security awareness, and the collaborative design of tools, rather than placing all security bets on a single model or system. Just as Ethereum has always upheld the value of decentralization, AI should exist as an auxiliary tool. Its goal is not to make decisions for people, but to help them make fewer mistakes. Looking back at the evolution of Web3 security, a clear trend emerges: early security was simply "keeping your mnemonic phrase safe," the mid-stage was "don't click on unfamiliar links, and promptly revoke invalid authorizations," and today, security is becoming a continuous, dynamic, and intelligent process. In this process, the introduction of AI has not diminished the significance of decentralization; on the contrary, it makes decentralized systems more suitable for long-term use by ordinary users. It hides complex risk analysis in the background, transforming key judgments into intuitive prompts presented to users, gradually turning security from an extra burden into a "default capability." This echoes my previous assessment: AI and Web3/Crypto are essentially a mirror image of the new era's "productive forces" and "relations of production" (further reading: "When Web3 Meets d/acc: What Can Crypto Do in the Era of Accelerated Technology?"): If we consider AI as an ever-evolving "spear"—which greatly improves efficiency but can also be used for large-scale malicious purposes—then the decentralized system built by Crypto is a "shield" that must evolve in tandem. From d/acc's perspective, the goal of this shield is not to create absolute security, but to ensure that, in the worst-case scenario, the system remains trustworthy, allowing users to have room to exit and save themselves. In conclusion, the ultimate goal of Web3 has never been to make users more technically savvy, but rather to enable technology to protect users without their awareness. Therefore, when attackers begin using AI, a defense system that refuses to be intelligent is itself a risk. This is why protecting assets is an endless game. In this era, users who know how to use AI to arm themselves will become the most difficult fortress to breach in this game. The significance of AI × Web3 may lie precisely here—not in creating absolute security, but in making security a capability that can be scaled up and replicated.

Partners

About Us

As a leading global blockchain industry service platform, Golden Finance covers the entire industry ecosystem, including information, data, research reports, and summits, always staying at the forefront of the industry. OracleX builds a next-generation decision support ecosystem connecting the real world and the digital economy, becoming an indispensable predictive data protocol layer and financial derivative infrastructure in the Web3.0 value internet era. data.title: Trump, rejected by JPMorgan Chase, is betting on another financial system.
data.description: A conflict spanning politics, finance, and personal destiny is unfolding between the Trump family and Wall Street. On January 18, Trump posted on his Truth Social media platform that he planned to sue JPMorgan Chase within the next two weeks, accusing it of "improper debanking" him after the 2021 Capitol Hill incident. On the surface, this appears to be a personal rights protection effort. However, what truly deserves attention is a recurring and evolving real-world question behind this conflict: where does a person go when excluded from the traditional financial system? A president becoming a "financial vagabond"? Let's rewind to January 2021. Following the Capitol Hill incident, the US financial system quickly entered a state of risk contraction. Many large banks, payment institutions, and custody service providers began proactively severing ties with "politically risky clients." Trump and his affiliated entities quickly became one of the most prominent targets. Trump subsequently accused JPMorgan Chase of demanding he transfer hundreds of millions of dollars out of his account within 20 days; when he tried to switch to other institutions such as Bank of America, he was also unable to obtain continued service. By 2025, this situation was publicly confirmed and amplified by members of the Trump family: several major banks, including JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America, and Capital One, were accused of terminating his banking account services without giving a clear reason. Eric Trump, Trump's second son, stated helplessly in an interview: "I never thought I would get involved in the crypto space until every bank started canceling our accounts for no reason, simply because of my father's political stance." The experience of being rejected by the traditional financial system became the "decisive moment" for the Trump family's shift to cryptocurrency. Starting from where they were rejected. But the Trump family wasn't content with simply "turning to crypto." For them, cryptocurrency wasn't a temporary escape from the financial system, but a path to re-enter the financial power structure. In 2024, with Trump's support, the DeFi project World Liberty Financial was launched. The project's positioning was extremely clear—not simply trading or speculating, but attempting to answer a real-world question: when traditional banks refuse to serve, who defines the financial gateway? World Liberty Financial's official statement is blunt—"Opening financial channels for everyone by replacing the restrictions set by traditional banks." Its stablecoin, USD1, quickly entered the top ranks of stablecoin market capitalization, becoming one of the core financial instruments in the system. But the truly crucial step occurred in early 2026. World Liberty Financial applied to regulators for a trust banking license, attempting to embed stablecoins directly into a compliant banking framework. In other words, they weren't just trying to "bypass banks," but to rebuild a controlled entry point within the banking system. Trump's two sons even openly stated at the Bitcoin 2025 conference that their endorsement of DeFi and Bitcoin stemmed from their experience of being "denied service" by banks. They positioned their family's crypto project as an alternative to the traditional financial system. They repeatedly emphasized that the traditional financial system had been "politicized" and "hostile," and it was this rejection that drove them to crypto assets. Therefore, this "revenge" of "turning to crypto" is not an escape from the financial system, but a reconstruction initiated from the point of rejection. The Shift in Financial Power Therefore, the truly important aspect of Trump's lawsuit against JPMorgan Chase is not the outcome of the case. Rather, it clearly reveals something to the market: when the traditional financial system begins to selectively close its doors, where will those shut out go? They will turn to a new system not controlled by a single financial institution. And the crypto world, precisely within this crack, has been repeatedly thrust into the forefront. This is not a victory for idealism, but rather the most sobering answer given by the friction between realpolitik and finance.

data.title: Venezuelan National Faces Up to 20 Years in Prison in Alleged $1B Crypto Laundering Scheme
data.description:
A global laundering network built on crypto rails

U.S. federal prosecutors have charged Jorge Figueira, a 59-year-old Venezuelan national, with orchestrating an alleged $1 billion crypto-enabled money-laundering operation, marking what authorities describe as one of the most significant cases of its kind ever pursued by the Justice Department.

Venezuelan Accused in Massive Crypto Laundering Operation

Jorge Figueira is facing federal charges for allegedly laundering nearly $1 billion through crypto and shell companies. Authorities say he ran a complex network that moved money across continents while hiding transactions… pic.twitter.com/Kny7JyMfjZ

— BABA CRYPTO (@babacryptoio) January 18, 2026

If convicted of conspiracy to launder money, Figueira faces up to 20 years in federal prison. The criminal complaint, filed in the Eastern District of Virginia, paints a picture of a highly coordinated global network designed to move vast sums of illicit cash through the crypto ecosystem while evading law enforcement scrutiny.

According to court filings, Figueira allegedly directed a sophisticated apparatus that converted cash into cryptocurrency, routed funds through hundreds of digital wallets, and then converted the assets back into fiat currency before distributing proceeds to recipients in high-risk jurisdictions, including Colombia, China, Panama, and Mexico.

Prosecutors say the scheme relied on a layered infrastructure of bank accounts across multiple countries, crypto exchange accounts for rapid conversions, private wallets for intermediate storage, and shell companies used to disguise the true origin and destination of funds. Subordinates allegedly executed hundreds of transactions designed to fragment money flows and obscure transaction trails.

The FBI’s Washington Field Office identified roughly $1 billion in cryptocurrency passing through wallets tied to the operation between 2018 and the present, with the majority of inbound funds originating from crypto trading platforms. Investigators say the scale and complexity of the operation allowed illicit capital to move efficiently across borders while remaining deliberately hidden from authorities.

The U.S. DOJ charged Venezuelan national Jorge Figueira with conspiring to launder around $1 billion in illicit funds through bank accounts, crypto exchanges, and private wallets. The probe, supported by the FBI, alleges extensive crypto-based transfers to conceal fund origins.…

— Wu Blockchain (@WuBlockchain) January 16, 2026

Justice Department highlights real-world harm

U.S. Attorney Lindsey Halligan underscored the broader implications of the case, warning that money laundering on this scale fuels transnational criminal activity. “Money laundering at this level enables criminal organizations to operate, expand, and inflict real-world harm,” she said, adding that those who move illicit funds in the billions “should expect to be identified, disrupted, and held fully accountable under federal law.”

The case against Figueira arrives as U.S. authorities ramp up enforcement against crypto-enabled financial crime, signaling a more aggressive posture toward networks exploiting digital assets for large-scale illicit activity.

Law enforcement officials and regulators have increasingly pointed to the growing scope of crypto-related crime. Earlier this week, Manhattan District Attorney Alvin Bragg urged New York lawmakers to criminalize unlicensed crypto operations, describing them as part of a $51 billion underground economy. The FBI reported nearly 11,000 crypto ATM-related complaints in 2024, totaling more than $246 million in losses, while blockchain analytics firm Chainalysis found that illicit addresses received a record $154 billion in 2025.

Recent prosecutions illustrate the breadth of enforcement efforts. In Utah, Brian Garry Sewell was sentenced to three years in prison for a $2.9 million fraud scheme, alongside operating an unlicensed cash-to-crypto business that converted more than $5.4 million in bulk cash. In New York, Ronald Spektor, a 23-year-old Brooklyn resident, was charged with stealing approximately $16 million from around 100 Coinbase users through phishing scams that relied on panic tactics rather than technical exploits.

As crypto seizures mount, the U.S. government has moved to formalize how confiscated digital assets are handled. Under an executive order signed by President Donald Trump, federal agencies established a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve, opting to retain seized cryptocurrency rather than auction it off. Current estimates place federal holdings at 328,372 BTC, valued at more than $31 billion.

Recent controversy emerged after reports suggested the Department of Justice had sold 57 Bitcoin forfeited by Samourai Wallet developers. However, White House crypto adviser Patrick Witt clarified on Friday that the assets were not liquidated and remain part of the reserve, in accordance with the executive order.

❌ A White House crypto advisor said the US government has not sold any Bitcoin forfeited in the Samourai Wallet case.#DOJ#Bitcoinhttps://t.co/pfX7fkilo8

— Cryptonews.com (@cryptonews) January 17, 2026

What comes next

For now, the charges against Figueira remain allegations, and he is presumed innocent until proven guilty. The case is being prosecuted by Assistant U.S. Attorney Catherine Rosenberg, with sentencing guidelines and statutory factors to be considered should a conviction be secured.

As the Justice Department continues to target large-scale crypto laundering networks, the Figueira case underscores a clear message from U.S. authorities: the anonymity of digital assets will not shield billion-dollar criminal operations from scrutiny or accountability.

data.title: South Korean Authorities Flag Record Crypto Laundering Case Using WeChat Pay, Alipay and Fake Expense Claims
data.description:
A sophisticated underground network that laundered roughly 148.9 billion won ($101.7 million) has been dismantled by the Korea Customs Service, exposing the complex tactics used to move illicit funds across borders.

The investigation reveals an operation that successfully bypassed financial watchdogs for nearly four years, highlighting the ongoing tug-of-war between digital innovation and financial security.

How Did The Criminal Ring Bypass Financial Borders?

Between September 2021 and June 2025, a trio of Chinese nationals allegedly orchestrated a massive laundering operation by “exploiting” a network of cryptocurrency accounts spanning multiple countries.

The group operated by collecting deposits from clients via popular digital payment platforms, specifically WeChat Pay and Alipay.

To stay under the radar, they purchased virtual assets in various international jurisdictions before funnelling them into South Korean digital wallets.

Once the crypto reached Korea, it was converted into local currency and dispersed through numerous domestic bank accounts to hide its origin.

귀화 중국인 일당, 1489억원 규모 가상자산 불법환치기 적발

해외 코인 매수 → 국내 환전·ATM 인출 수법 사용
성형외과 결제 등 자금세탁 루트 다수 활용
세관, 주범 검거·공범 추적 중
이용 고객까지 수사 확대 예정 pic.twitter.com/lKwcpJh3uL

— 눈떴 (@Awaken_Eyes) January 19, 2026

Were Legitimate Fees Used As A Front For Laundering?

The group’s strategy relied heavily on creating a paper trail of seemingly innocent transactions.

Authorities discovered that the laundered money was frequently disguised as legitimate expenses, such as cosmetic surgery fees for foreign visitors or tuition funds for students studying abroad.

These justifications allowed the ring to move significant sums without immediately triggering alarms.

Korea Cracks Down: $102M Crypto Laundering Ring 🏛⛓️

Major enforcement action in Seoul (Jan 19, 2026):

🔹 The Bust: 150B won ($101.7M) laundered via unauthorized FX schemes.

🔹 The Tactic: Disguising crypto flows as medical fees and study costs. #Crypto#MoneyLaundering

— CoinTrend | Crypto News (@CoinTrendNews) January 19, 2026

Beyond these specific covers, the suspects also moved funds under the guise of trade fees and duty-free purchase costs, creating a smokescreen of everyday commerce to mask the movement of the funds.

Is South Korea Winning The War On Suspicious Transactions?

The crackdown comes at a time when South Korean authorities are seeing a massive spike in flagged activity.

In 2025 alone, local virtual asset service providers filed a record 36,684 suspicious transaction reports between January and August.

This figure is particularly striking as it exceeds the combined total of the previous two years.

South Korea flagged a record of 36,684 suspicious #crypto transactions in 2025, surpassing the total of the past two years.

A majority involve illegal foreign exchange remittances, with stablecoins like USDT at the center. pic.twitter.com/YM1KhVVfVH

— Crypto Hawk (@CryptoKelvin12) September 22, 2025

In response to this surge, the government is closing existing loopholes, including a new mandate to tighten oversight on transfers below 1 million won.

This move is specifically designed to prevent users from evading identity checks through small, frequent transactions.

What Happens To The Suspects Now?

Following the investigation, the three Chinese nationals have been referred to the prosecution for alleged violations of the Foreign Exchange Transactions Act.

The Korea Customs Service (KCS) remains on high alert as the nation continues its transition into a global crypto hub.

While Seoul has spent much of the past year embracing digital assets at a rapid pace, the customs office noted that maintaining rigorous security is “critical for the expanding industry.”

This latest bust serves as a primary example of the increased scrutiny facing the sector as regulators move to align crypto standards with traditional banking safety.

data.title: $282 Million Lost From Crypto Wallet in Social Engineering Scam Triggers Monero Surge
data.description:
Crypto Wallet Loses Over $280 Million In Elaborate Social Engineering Attack

A high-value cryptocurrency wallet became the target of a sophisticated social engineering scam, resulting in the theft of more than $282 million in Bitcoin (BTC) and Litecoin (LTC).

The incident, first flagged by popular on-chain investigator ZachXBT, did not involve any protocol exploits, software flaws, or zero-day vulnerabilities.

Instead, the attacker gained access by manipulating the wallet owner through an elaborate deception.

How Did Hackers Evade Security Measures

The breach unfolded shortly before midnight UTC on 10 January, when the wallet suddenly became active.

Within hours, its vast holdings, including a substantial Litecoin balance and four-figure Bitcoin holdings, were in motion.

On January 10, 2026 at around 11 pm UTC a victim lost $282M+ worth of LTC & BTC due to a hardware wallet social engineering scam.

The attacker began converting the stolen LTC & BTC to Monero via multiple instant exchanges causing the XMR price to sharply increase.

BTC was also…

— ZachXBT (@zachxbt) January 16, 2026

The attacker moved quickly, using instant swap services and cross-chain bridges designed for speed rather than transparency.

By rapidly converting and scattering assets, the thief reduced the chances of interception or freezing.

Monero Surges Amid Laundering Activity

A significant portion of the stolen funds was converted into Monero (XMR), a privacy-focused cryptocurrency.

At the time of the transfer, XMR was trading near $450.

Within days, the privacy coin experienced consecutive all-time highs, peaking around $800 on 15 January.

XMR grew from $450 to $800 due to a hacker's money laundering 🤯

On January 10, a major social engineering attack resulted in the loss of over $282M in BTC and LTC

The attacker laundered the stolen funds by converting them to #Monero, leading to further retail purchases and an… pic.twitter.com/7uWkpAPD8I

— Web3_Vibes (@W3Vibes) January 17, 2026

Analysts noted that the price surge reflected forced liquidity demand rather than organic buying, suggesting laundering pressure.

Since the peak, XMR has retraced below $630, likely as attackers offloaded portions of the proceeds.

THORChain Posts Stir Controversy In Crypto Community

Further tracing revealed that a large portion of the stolen BTC was bridged into Ethereum, Ripple (XRP), and additional Litecoin via THORChain.

The platform’s posts on X drew swift reactions, with some community members accusing its social media team of “celebrating” criminal activity.

Others highlighted the risks of social engineering scams, warning that even hardware wallets are vulnerable when victims are manipulated by convincing fake identities.

Such scams often involve impersonation, including fake accounts claiming sudden romantic interest, even when the parties have never met.

Cross-Chain Laundering Complicates Investigation

Blockchain analysis showed that the stolen assets were fragmented across multiple chains, increasing the difficulty of forensic tracking.

Hundreds of BTC were routed through THORChain and converted into a mix of assets, slowing attribution and complicating investigations.

Analysis of the threat actor’s wallets on the blockchain shows that a significant portion of the stolen funds was swapped from BTC to ETH via @THORChain bridge. This caused unusual spikes in the protocol’s overall volumes, drawing global attention to the case.

— Whitestream - Blockchain Intelligence (@whitestream5) January 17, 2026

The case illustrates how cross-chain infrastructure, while legitimate for ordinary users, can also facilitate rapid movement and obfuscation of large-scale illicit funds.

No Signs Of State-Linked Involvement

Despite the magnitude of the theft, investigators found no evidence linking the attack to state-sponsored hacking groups that have been involved in previous high-profile crypto crimes.

The identity of the wallet owner remains undisclosed, and it is unclear whether the wallet belonged to a single individual or an institutional holder.

Threat Actor #LTC wallet: ltc1qly43c2prj4c2e85dcspzpjd36jnapnenldnr70 ltc1qpg8zkv7y7lpu70sqxkdz9sjgu3aujy9l2pkqe7

— Whitestream - Blockchain Intelligence (@whitestream5) January 17, 2026

Social Engineering Remains A High-Risk Threat

This incident serves as a reminder that social engineering scams remain one of the most dangerous threats to cryptocurrency holders.

Even hardware wallets, widely considered the most secure option for storing digital assets, are vulnerable if attackers successfully manipulate their owners.

ZachXBT’s investigation has shed light on the sophisticated methods used to bypass these safeguards, demonstrating the speed and complexity of modern crypto theft.
